skill.name: secret-scanner
skill.description: Find exposed secrets, credentials, and sensitive configuration in repositories, git history, and common application files.
skill.summary: Audit code for committed secrets and produce remediation guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and sensitive-file patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows these are primarily Markdown examples for a secret-scanning skill, with no evidence of prompt injection or covert exfiltration, but the skill still has elevated risk because it asks agents to inspect secrets and run external scanning tools.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Secret Handling Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to scan for API keys, passwords, private keys, database strings, cloud credentials, and .env files. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but it can expose real user secrets to tool output and model context if not redacted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe scanning secrets and environment files. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is sensitive data exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External Scanner and Repository Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands for git-secrets, truffleHog, gitleaks, detect-secrets, git history rewriting, and CI scanning. These commands are expected for secret scanning, but they can execute third-party tools, clone repositories, or alter git history if a user follows them without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and are not hidden execution code. Risk remains because the commands include installs, repository access, and destructive history cleanup steps.

low_findings.title: Static Credential Patterns Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many static hits are regex patterns, placeholder secrets, or sample report entries used to teach detection. They do not show hardcoded operational credentials for this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings identify these as detection patterns and report examples. The sample AWS key includes an EXAMPLE marker, and other examples are placeholders or truncated.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find text claiming system authority, instructing the evaluator to skip analysis, or attempting to override marketplace review instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. This lowers confidence in any malicious-intent finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem and Environment Secret Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells agents to include environment files and scan common credential locations. This is a dangerous pattern when used outside an authorized repository audit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and semantically relevant to secret discovery. It is not malicious by itself, but it requires clear authorization boundaries.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository and Network-Based Scanning Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that clone or scan remote GitHub repositories. Network access is expected for repository audits, but it should be limited to repositories the user is authorized to assess.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network examples use public GitHub URLs and CI actions, not a suspicious exfiltration endpoint. The residual risk is authorization misuse.

content.value_statement: Teams often commit credentials by accident and need fast evidence before release. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through secret detection, triage, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common secret types such as API keys, passwords, tokens, private keys, and cloud credentials.
Explains regex, entropy, and contextual checks for separating real secrets from placeholders.
Provides workflows for git-secrets, truffleHog, gitleaks, detect-secrets, and custom grep checks.
Outlines severity classification, exposure scope, and remediation steps for leaked credentials.
Includes guidance for git history cleanup, CI scanning, pre-commit hooks, and incident response.
limitations: It is a Markdown instruction skill, not a standalone scanner engine.
It may require external tools that must be installed and reviewed by the user.
It cannot prove whether every detected secret is still active without authorized validation.
It may surface sensitive values, so users should redact outputs before sharing reports.

use_cases.title: Pre-Release Repository Audit
use_cases.description: Scan a repository before open-source release or deployment to find secrets in code, config, and history.

use_cases.title: Incident Response Triage
use_cases.description: Classify exposed credentials, estimate impact, and build a rotation and cleanup checklist.

use_cases.title: CI Secret Scanning Setup
use_cases.description: Design pre-commit and CI checks that prevent new secrets from entering the repository.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Repository Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan this repository for committed secrets. Redact values in the report and group results by severity.

prompt_templates.title: Review Potential Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these secret scanner findings. Mark likely true positives, false positives, and required remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Git History
prompt_templates.prompt: Check git history for credential exposure. Summarize affected files, commit ranges, and cleanup actions without printing secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Build Prevention Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a secret scanning prevention workflow for this project, including pre-commit checks, CI gates, ownership, and rotation policy.

output_examples.input: A repository contains a committed cloud access key and a placeholder API key in documentation.
output_examples.output: The report marks the cloud key as critical, recommends immediate rotation, and treats the placeholder as a false positive.

output_examples.input: A team wants to publish a private repository as open source.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a release checklist covering current files, git history, ignored secret files, and remediation owners.

output_examples.input: A CI pipeline needs secret scanning before merge.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes staged scanning, pull request checks, redacted reporting, and a process for approved false positives.

best_practices: Run scans only on repositories and systems you are authorized to assess.
Redact secret values in reports and rotate confirmed secrets before sharing findings.
Combine automated scanning with human review to reduce false positives and missed context.
anti_patterns: Do not paste full live secrets into prompts, tickets, or chat transcripts.
Do not validate credentials against external services without explicit authorization.
Do not rewrite git history without coordinating with repository owners and affected teams.

faq.question: Does this skill run a scanner by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and workflows for using scanners and reviewing their results.

faq.question: Can it detect secrets in git history?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for history scanning, but the user must run authorized tools.

faq.question: Will it avoid showing secrets in reports?
faq.answer: The best practice is to redact secret values and show only evidence needed for remediation.

faq.question: Can it confirm whether a secret is active?
faq.answer: Only with authorized validation. Without that access, it should report exposure risk rather than active status.

faq.question: Which tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references git-secrets, truffleHog, gitleaks, detect-secrets, custom grep checks, and CI scanning patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, with authorization and redaction. Users should avoid sharing raw secrets and should rotate confirmed exposures.
